title: Night Lane
desc: When the false sea cools after sunset, travelers cross it by stars, oil cups, and patience.

steps.text:
By noon, Rao'Pa Sahkk can make a skilled traveler doubt their own feet. By night, it becomes almost kind. The heat lifts, the shimmer collapses, and the flats turn from false ocean into a dark open plain under enough stars to make direction feel possible again.

That is when the night lanes open. Low ceramic cups are filled with oil and hooded so the flame shows only from the correct approach. Brass star markers catch moonlight where fire would be too obvious. A dusk caravan is due within the hour, and half the route still needs preparing.

The lane captain kneels beside a crate of cups and a bundle of marker rods. "Light line or star line," she says. "Pick one. Both have to be right before the first sled reaches the flats."

options.label: Fill and hood the ceramic light cups
Set the brass star markers along the outer curve

steps.text:
The oil cups sit low to the ground so wind cannot toy with them and distant eyes cannot mistake them for campfires. You fill each one, settle the narrow hood over the lip, and touch flame to wick just long enough to catch. From the wrong angle, the cup disappears entirely. From the lane, it becomes a steady amber point no traveler can misread.

As darkness deepens, the line of cups begins to draw itself across the flats like a patient constellation made by hand. The false sea of the day is gone. In its place is a silent black expanse held together by careful little lights and the discipline of creatures who know exactly how much emptiness lies between them.

When the caravan finally appears, it moves toward the cups with visible relief. One driver lifts a hand in thanks without slowing. The captain watches the line hold and smiles into the dark. "Best time to cross," she murmurs. Looking out over the lights, you understand why.

options.label: Keep the cups burning until the last sled passes

steps.text:
The star markers are thin rods capped with hammered brass shapes cut to catch the least available light. Crescent, point, fork, circle. Each pattern means something to the travelers who use the lane: hold straight, bend west, shallow drift ahead, camp within sight by dawn.

You walk the outer curve with the captain, setting each rod by measured pace and checking the angle against the sky before pressing it home. Once the line is complete, the markers seem to hang in the dark rather than stand in it, bright enough to guide but too spare to spoil the size of the night.

By the time the caravan arrives, the cups and brass signs work together like two languages saying the same thing. Stay here. Keep moving. The road is real even if the land prefers pretending otherwise. In Rao'Pa Sahkk, guidance is an art made from very small certainties.

options.label: Watch the brass points take the moon
